output:
**(C)** is the answer that best reflects structural reality.

The key insight is that **the same mathematical object changes category depending on its instantiation**:

- **As published source code or a paper** — it functions as expression. You can read it, critique it, teach it, build on it intellectually. Restricting its publication is structurally equivalent to restricting a math textbook. This is why *Bernstein v. DOJ* (9th Circuit, 1999) found that source code is protected speech.

- **As compiled, deployed, operational software** — it functions as a tool. A running AES implementation in a weapons system or a sanctions-evading communication platform has direct operational capability. Regulating its *export in executable form* is structurally closer to regulating the export of any other dual-use technology.

Why the other options fail:

- **(A)** collapses the distinction — it treats the algorithm itself as a munition, which would make publishing a math paper an arms export.
- **(B)** also collapses the distinction from the other direction — it ignores that deployed encryption software genuinely does have operational military/intelligence capability beyond mere "expression."
- **(D)** dodges the question entirely and doesn't resolve the structural ambiguity.

The real-world resolution (post-*Bernstein*, post-EAR reforms) largely converged on something close to (C): published/open-source cryptographic code got largely decontrolled, while export of specific operational cryptographic products to embargoed destinations remained restricted.
